feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
feed.subtitle: SAP Cloud Application Programming Model blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: MTA and PNPM combined
entry.content:
When working with a monorepository for your BTP CloudFoundry application, using a package manager like pnpm can be highly beneficial. However, switching to a different package manager requires adapting your build and deployment configurations, specifically in the mta.yaml file for BTP CloudFoundry. With pnpm, each UI component and CAP service are managed as separate packages, unlike a typical CAP application where everything is contained within a single application with various UI components in the App folder. With pnpm, the structure would look more like this:

  • Package folders: the main folder with each UI Component and CAP application (could be more then one).

This structure is a bit different compared to a typical CAP application and requires some changes in the configuration files.

In the mta.yaml file, you'll need to adjust the before-all build commands. Instead of using npm install and cds build, you'll need to replace these with equivalent pnpm commands. Specifically, use pnpm to execute npm install within the CAP package by applying a filter with the pnpm command. Similarly, update the build command to leverage pnpm.

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L174

 

The pnpm build script will execute the build script in the cap package which is in the packages folder:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L28

 

The mta.yaml configuration for the CAP module is normally configured to use “npm” as builder. It is not possible to change this to pnpm. This might cause problems when your CAP services has dependencies to other pnpm packages. Anyway, keep “npm” as builder:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L21

 

The npm build  script of the CAP project (in before-all of mta.yaml) copies the package.json including the devDependencies which cannot be resolved when it contains references to other packages. It cannot resolve the dependencies because the workspace references can only be resolved by pnpm. The default mta build uses npm for the service and cannot be changed to pnpm:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L11

 

As a solution you have to install the npm package  “cds-plugin-ui5” to the CAP app, which will remove the workspace dependencies when building. Special thanks to @pmuessig for this plugin:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L9

 

For the UI packages, the mta.yaml configuration needs to be adapted as well. In all html5 modules, the commands “npm install” and “npm run build:cf” have to be replaced with pnpm commands:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L58

 

The filter will be used to run the command in the related package. The name in the filter is the name of the package in the package.json:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/app/package.json#L2

 

With that, you’re monorepository with multiple packages CAP and UI should be good to go! You can find the full Github repository here: https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env

entry.title: Error: Cannot find module '@sap/hana-client' in CAPM Project
entry.content:
Hi,

entry.title: Use SAP CAP SAPUI5 Smart Filter to Show Value Help that are not Associated Entitites
entry.content:
How to Use SAP CAP and SAPUI5 Smart Filter to Show Value Help for Fields that are not Associated Entities

In cases where you need to allow the user to select values through a Value Help for a specific field that is not related to another table, you can achieve this behavior in the following way:

1. Create a View that Contains the Values for the Value Help

First, you need to create a view that only contains the values you want to display in the Value Help. This view should perform a SELECT on the entity from which you need to retrieve the values and use a GROUP BY to avoid repeating information.

 

@CDS.odata.valuelist
view Modules as
    select from GlobalSetting {
        key GlobalSettingModule
    }
    where
        GlobalSettingModule is not null
    group by
        GlobalSettingModule;

 

2. Add @Common Annotations in the Main View

In the view, entity, or projection that you are using to build the Smart Table, you need to add @Common annotations. These annotations will serve as input for CAP to determine that the previously created view should be used for the Value Help.

 

view ViewGlobalSettingPatterns as
    select from GlobalSetting {
        *,
        @Common.ValueList.CollectionPath: 'Module'
        @Common.ValueList.Parameters    : [{
            $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
            LocalDataProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule',
            ValueListProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule'
        }, {
            $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
            ValueListProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule'
        }]
        GlobalSettingModule
    }
    where
        GlobalSettingClassification = 'PATTERN';

 

3. Expose the Views in the Service

You need to expose the new views so that the service can access them and use them as a data source.

 

service MasterDataService {
    entity GlobalSetting             as projection on model.GlobalSetting;
    @readonly
    entity ViewGlobalSettingPatterns as projection on model.ViewGlobalSettingPatterns;
    @readonly
    @CDS.odata.valuelist
    entity Type                      as projection on model.Types;
    @readonly
    entity Module                    as projection on model.Modules;
}

 

4. Verify the OData Metadata

It is important to verify that the metadata correctly shows the annotations. The CollectionPath is the key point since it tells CAP which resource will be used to display the data in the Value Help.

The metadata should look something like this:

 

<Annotations xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Target="MasterDataService.ViewGlobalSettingPatterns/GlobalSettingModule">
    <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
        <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
            <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Module"/>
            <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                <Collection>
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                        <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                    </Record>
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                    </Record>
                </Collection>
            </PropertyValue>
        </Record>
    </Annotation>
</Annotations>

 

5. Configure the Filter in the Smart Component

Finally, you just need to configure the Smart Filter, where you specify which field will display the Value Help.

 

<smartFilterBar:ControlConfiguration
    key="GlobalSettingModule"
    label="Module"
    visibleInAdvancedArea="true"
    hasValueHelpDialog="true"/>

 

 

Result

Anthony Ramos Romero.

entry.title: Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account
entry.content:
How to Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account or Subaccount Without HDI

When your SAP HANA Cloud database is in another Global Account or subaccount, which prevents the natural use of the HDI container for database object management, you need to follow the steps below to configure your SAP CAP application and achieve a successful connection.

1: Configure a User-Provided Service

It is essential to create a user-provided service using the Cloud Foundry command line. This is important because SAP CAP requires the service to have three specific tags to automatically determine which configuration to use.

Run the following command:

cf create-user-provided-service ups-database-external -t "hana,database,relational"
  • ups-database-external: This is the name you assign to your service.
  • -t "hana,database,relational": These are the tags that CAP uses to identify the database service.

2: Modify the Service's JSON in SAP BTP

After creating the service via the command line, you need to specify the connection data in the service's JSON in SAP BTP.

The JSON should contain the following:

{
  "encrypt": true,
  "host": "your-hana-cloud-host",
  "password": "your-password",
  "port": "443",
  "schema": "YOUR_SCHEMA_NAME",
  "sslValidateCertificate": true,
  "user": "your-username"
}
  • host: The address of your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • user and password: The credentials of the user with permissions to access the database schema.
  • schema: The schema where your database objects are located or will be created.
  • encrypt and sslValidateCertificate: Settings to ensure the connection is secure via SSL/TLS.
  • port: The connection port, generally "443" for secure connections.

3: Modify package.json or .cdsrc.json

You need to tell SAP CAP which service to use to obtain the credentials. This is done by modifying your project's package.json or .cdsrc.json file.

Add or update the "requires" section as follows:

{
  "requires": {
    "db": {
      "kind": "hana",
      "credentials": {
        "service": "ups-database-external"
      }
    },
    "[production]": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "db": {
        "kind": "hana",
        "credentials": {
          "service": "ups-database-external"
        }
      },
      "data": false
    }
  }
}
  • "service": "ups-database-external": Indicates to CAP that it should use the provided service named ups-database-external.
  • "[production]": Ensures that the same configuration is used in the production profile.

4: Remove HDI-Related Configurations

Since you are not using HDI for database object management, you need to remove all references and configurations related to HDI in your mta.yaml file or your project's deployment configurations.

This includes:

  • Removing modules or resources that create or manage HDI containers.
  • Deleting dependencies between modules and HDI resources.

5: Bind the Service to the CAP Application Instance in SAP BTP

For your CAP application to take the specified credentials and configuration, you need to bind the provided service to your application's instance in SAP BTP.

You can do this in two ways:

Option 1: Manual Binding

After deploying the application, run the command:

cf bind-service your-application-name ubs-database-external

Then, restart your application:

cf restart your-application-name

Option 2: Automatic Binding in mta.yaml

Add the binding in your mta.yaml file so that it is performed automatically during deployment.

Example of how to define the binding in mta.yaml:

modules:
  - name: your-application-name
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    requires:
      - name: ups-database-external
resources:
  - name: ups-database-external
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: user-provided
      service-name: ups-database-external

6: Manually Deploy the Database Objects

Since you are not using HDI, you need to manually deploy the database objects in your SAP HANA Cloud instance.

1. Generate the SQL Scripts

From your CDS models, generate the SQL scripts using the following commands:

cds compile db/ --to sql --src db cds compile srv/ --to sql --src srv

These commands generate SQL scripts from your model definitions in the db/ and srv/ directories.

2. Execute the Scripts in the Database Console

  • Access SAP HANA Database Explorer.
  • Connect to your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • Execute the generated SQL scripts to create the necessary tables and views in your schema.

Anthony Ramos Romero

entry.title: XSUAA Local Testing
entry.content:
Let’s quickly dive into the concept.
How To Add Authentication and Authorization:

To enhance the security of our data, we integrate the XSUAA service into our CAP project. XSUAA is the default authentication mechanism in CAP.

You can add XSUAA to a CAP project in two ways: either by selecting the XSUAA option when creating the project, or by manually adding it later through commands.

Adding the XSUAA service generates an xs-security.json file and creates a resource entry in the mta.yaml file.

In this blog, I will explain how to add XSUAA to a CAP project, including how to set it up when creating the project.

Step 1:
Create a new project and include XSUAA during the project setup.
After the project is created, you will get an xs-security.json file that includes scopes, attributes, and role templates. If the file does not contain xsappname or tenant-mode, add them manually. Ensure that xsappname is properly configured and tenant-mode is set to "dedicated." The xsappname in the xs-security.json file must match the name you provide in the mta.yaml file, which is usually the project name.

xs-security.json file looks like below after project creation

{ 
"tenant-mode":"dedicated", 
"xsappname":"organization", 
"scopes": [], 
"attributes": [], 
"role-templates": [], 
"authorities-inheritance": false 
}

Step 2:
Create the domain model and service.
Once the project is created, you can start developing the domain model. Create entities inside the .cds file that you set up in the db folder. For example, in my case, the file path is db/org_db.cds.
db/org_db.cds

namespace com.cy.organization;

entity Employees {
    key Id          : String;
        name        : String;
        designation : String;
        salary      : String;
        payslips    : Association to Payslips;
}

entity Payslips {
    key Id          : String;
        name        : String;
        monthly_ctc : String;
        employees   : Association to many Employees
                          on employees.payslips = $self;
}

Create a service file inside the srv folder to expose your entities as a service. For example, I created srv/org_service.cds.
srv/org_service.cds.

using com.cy.organization as cy from '../db/ org_db';
service mySrv{
    entity Employees as projection on cy.Employees;
    entity Payslips as projection on cy.Payslips;
}

Now, add some mock data using the cds add data command. Initially, set authentication to "mocked" in the package.json file. Then, do cds build and deploy and run cds watch to check if your service is working correctly.
Here is the employee csv data db/data/com.cy.organization-Employees.csv:

Id, Name, Designation, Salary, Payslips_Id
1, John Doe, Software Engineer, 5000, 101
2, Jane Smith, Project Manager, 6000, 102
3, Alice Johnson, Business Analyst, 7000, 103
4, Bob Brown, Data Scientist, 8000, 104

Here is the paysleeps csv data com.cy.organization-Payslips.csv

Id, Name, Monthly_CTC
1, John Doe, 5000
2, Jane Smith, 6000
3, Alice Johnson, 7000
4, Bob Brown, 8000

Step 3:
Add scopes, roles, and entity-level restrictions based on the defined roles and scopes in your service file.
Add scopes, roles to xs-security.json file
A security file will have three components: scopes, attributes, and role-templates.
Scopes:
Scopes define who can access what within the CAP services. For example, an admin can view all employee details, while a specific employee can only see their own details. In this section, we define the access scope for each user. Below, we will create scopes in the xs-security.json file.
Example Scopes:
Here are the scopes I have declared in my xs-security.json file:

"scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.MANAGER",
      "description": "Manager Scope"
    },
    {
      "name":"$XSAPPNAME.EMPLOYEE",
      "description":"employee scope"
    }
  ]

Attributes:
Attributes are used to restrict access to specific columns within the entities. By defining attributes, you can control which data fields a user can view or modify based on their assigned roles.
Role-Templates:
Roles differ from scopes in that a role can encompass multiple scopes. For example, if you are logged in with a manager role, that manager can have access to multiple scopes, allowing them to perform various actions based on their permissions.
Example Role-Templates:

"role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "Manager_Role",
      "description": "manager role",
      "scope-references": [
        "$XSAPPNAME.MANAGER"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name":"Employee_Role",
      "description":"employee role",
      "scope-references":[
        "$XSAPPNAME.EMPLOYEE"
      ]
    }
  ]

After creating roles and scopes, your xs-security.json file will look like this:

{
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "xsappname": "organization",
  "scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.MANAGER",
      "description": "Manager Scope"
    },
    {
      "name":"$XSAPPNAME.EMPLOYEE",
      "description":"employee scope"
    }
  ],
  "attributes": [],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "Manager_Role",
      "description": "manager role",
      "scope-references": [
        "$XSAPPNAME.MANAGER"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name":"Employee_Role",
      "description":"employee role",
      "scope-references":[
        "$XSAPPNAME.EMPLOYEE"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "authorities-inheritance": false
}

Entity Level Restrictions According to Roles and Scopes in the Service File:
Once the XSUAA setup is complete and roles and scopes are defined, you can apply entity-level restrictions in your service file. This restricts the operations that logged-in users can perform based on their roles and scopes.

Grant:
A "grant" defines the types of operations a user can perform on an entity (such as create, read, update, or delete). In the example below, we restrict certain operations without limiting access to the data itself.

After adding entity-level restrictions based on the roles and scopes, my srv/org_service.cds file will look like this:

using com.cy.organization as cy from '../db/ org_db';

service mySrv @(requries:'authenticated-user') {
    entity Employees @(restrict: [
        {
            grant: ['*'],
            to   : 'Manager_Role'
        },
        {
            grant: ['READ'],
            to   : 'Employee_Role'
        }
    ]) as projection on cy.Employees;

    entity Payslips as projection on cy.Payslips;
}

The above code indicates that users with the Manager role (via AdminScope) can perform all CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) on the Employees entity, while users with the Employee role (via EmployeeScope) are limited to only the Read operation.

Step 4:
Testing Authentication and Authorization Locally:
When testing authentication locally, we need to set up mock users in the package.json file. Typically, authentication and authorization are used on the deployed URL, but for local testing, we can mock this setup.

To do this, make changes in the package.json file by adding profiles inside the cds.requires section. First, add the auth property inside the cds.requires section, where you can create profiles for XSUAA strategies—one for development (local testing) and one for production. Also, add the db section to define profiles for SQLite (local testing) and HANA (production), as shown below:

"cds": {
    "requires": {
     "auth":{
      "[production]":{
        "strategy":"JWT"
      },
      "[development]":{
        "strategy":"mock"
       
      }
     },
     "db":{
      "[production]":{
        "kind": "hana"
      },
      "[development]":{
        "kind": "sqlite"
      }
     }
    }
  }

After making the changes, adding mock users, and including profiles for db and auth, my package.json file looks like this:

"cds": {
    "requires": {
     "auth":{
      "[production]":{
        "strategy":"JWT"
      },
      "[development]":{
        "strategy":"mock",
        "users": {
          "Jane Smith@gmail.com": {
            "password": "Jane",
            "roles": [ "Manager_Role"]
          },
          "John Doe@gmail.com": {
            "password": "John",
            "roles": [ "Employee_Role"]
          }
        }
      }
     },
     "db":{
      "[production]":{
        "kind": "hana"
      },
      "[development]":{
        "kind": "sqlite"
      }
     }
    }
  }

After modifying the package.json file, run the project locally using the cds watch command. Once the application is running, try accessing a specific entity. It will prompt you to enter a username and password.

To test whether the authentication and authorization are working correctly, try entering a random username that is not listed in the mock users. If everything is set up properly, access will be denied.

Once you attempt to log in with a random username, you'll encounter the following error:

Now, use the username and password of one of the mock users defined in the package.json file. For example, use the admin or employee credentials. After logging in successfully, you'll be able to see the output as shown below:

Note:
In this example, authentication and authorization are applied only to the Employees entity. So, while testing the application locally, ensure you're checking the authentication and authorization specifically for the Employees entity.

That's all about testing XSUAA locally!

entry.title: Authorization Management Service in SAP Cloud Identity Service
entry.content:
SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) is transitioning from using XSUAA for authorization management to a more integrated solution with Authorization Management Service (AMS). AMS is a pivotal component within the SAP Cloud Identity Service, designed to streamline and centralize the management of end-user authorizations for applications. This service ensures that individuals have appropriate access to the resources and functionalities they need to perform their roles effectively while mitigating security risks.

Firstly, many applications define authorizations internally to meet their specific domain needs. These internal mechanisms ensure tailored security but can lead to a decentralized and fragmented access management system. To address this, SAP Cloud Identity Services offers a solution by centralizing user assignments to roles and groups, significantly simplifying the management of user access.

The Authorization Management Service builds on this by providing a unified platform for managing authorizations across applications within SAP BTP. AMS integrates seamlessly with SAP Cloud Identity Services, enabling administrators to configure and assign policies through a centralized console. This integration enhances the efficiency and consistency of policy application across different services and users.

At the core of AMS is policy assignment. Within SAP Cloud Identity, each policy aligns with a group in the Identity Directory, and these policies can be linked to users by assigning them to the appropriate group. SAP offers flexibility in this process, allowing customers to use both predefined SAP policies and custom policies, which can be managed either through the Cloud Identity console or programmatically via the SCIM API. This ensures a scalable and adaptable authorization management system tailored to organizational needs. 

 

By centralizing authorization management, AMS offers several key benefits:

  • Improved Efficiency: Streamlined processes for assigning and managing authorizations reduce administrative overhead.
  • Enhanced Security: Centralized control over access helps prevent unauthorized access and data breaches.
  • Simplified Compliance: AMS can assist organizations in meeting regulatory requirements related to data privacy and security.
  • Flexibility: Customers can choose from pre-defined SAP policies or create custom policies to suit their specific needs.

What is a policy? 

A policy in the context of Authorization Management Service (AMS) is a structured set of rules that governs whether a user or group of users is allowed to perform specific actions on a resource, depending on certain conditions. These conditions can be based on user attributes or data from the business application.

In AMS, policies externalize authorization decisions to the AMS runtime engine, which evaluates relevant policies for a particular authorization request. Each policy defines the type of access (such as "Read") to a specific resource (like a business object) and is evaluated at runtime based on conditions set by administrators, who can adjust restrictions as needed. This allows for flexible and dynamic control over resource access within business applications.

Here are some example policies in Data Control Language (DCL) with actions on special resources where additionally attribute values restrict the user authorization. 

In the following sections, we will delve deeper into the functionalities of AMS, including its integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services, policy assignment mechanisms, and how it contributes to overall application security. 

Authorization Management for Cloud Identity Service

The Authorization Management Service (AMS) is now integrated into the user management console of SAP Cloud Identity, enabling customers to implement fine-grained access control for delegated administration. This feature allows organizations to restrict the authorizations of user management administrators to a specific subset of users or attributes within the user base. As a result, customers can delegate administrative tasks to local or specialized administrators without granting full access to all user data.

In some cases, the default administrator authorizations provided in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant may be too broad, offering unrestricted data access. For scenarios requiring more nuanced control, such as attribute-based access control (ABAC), customers can define custom authorization models with detailed instance-level restrictions. Administrators create authorization policies using user attributes and assign these policies to other administrators, enabling one administrator to manage only a specific subset of users or user attributes. Similarly, administrators can define policies using application organization IDs to restrict access to certain applications.

By leveraging policies, customers can configure precise access control for SAP Cloud Identity Services administrators. To activate this feature, it must first be enabled via the SAP Cloud Identity admin console. Once enabled, the "Authorization Policies" tab will appear within approximately 60 seconds in the administration console.

Initially, only the base policies are visible. You can create new authorization policies on the base policies and assign them to administrators. The custom policies will also appear in the list along with the base policies. 

1. Configure User Authorizations

Let me show you an example that how to define a policy and assign the policy to an administrator. Sue Smith is an administrator of SAP Cloud Identity Services, and she is responsible for the branch office in the USA. As such she needs to have access only to the users in the USA. When you create a new policy, you can restrict the users on the basis of the following attributes: user.name, country, costCenter, division, department, and organization. In this example, you can choose country to restrict management of the user only in the USA.

Note: The addresses must be marked as primary via the Identity Directory SCIM REST API. Users who don't have a primary address are excluded even if the user.addresses.country attribute matches the address of the user.

 Once the rule definition is completed, you can assign this policy to Sue Smith. 

Last but not least, you also need to remove the Read Users and Manage Users authorizations that Sue has as an administrator. 

2. Configure Application Authorizations

You can also configure new policies to allow the administrator manage a subset of applications in SAP Cloud Identity Services. When you create a new policy, you can restrict the users on the basis of the attribute application.organization. By default, all applications are in the global organization. You can edit the Organization ID of the application and define a custom policy. 

Authorization Management for Custom Business Applications

The Authorization Management Service (AMS) as part of SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI) provides libraries and tools that enable developers of cloud business applications to define, enforce, and manage instance-based authorization checks. When integrated with the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model, AMS policies can include CAP roles along with additional filter criteria for instance-based authorizations that can be defined in the CAP model. These authorizations are then transformed into AMS policies, which can later be refined by authorization administrators in the SCI administration console and assigned to business users.

For developers, managing authorizations is a crucial but often complex task. Proper authorization ensures that end users have controlled access to critical data and functionality, protecting the company from potential harm caused by mistakes or malicious actions. However, finding the right balance of access control can be challenging and time-consuming.

The new Authorization Management Service simplifies this process for developers working on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). AMS provides a centralized system for managing end-user authorizations for business applications built on SAP BTP. It seamlessly integrates with SAP's development models, particularly the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model. When defining an authorization policy, developers can specify the conditions under which access is granted and whether these conditions can be adjusted later. Once the application is deployed on SAP BTP, administrators can further customize these authorization policies with specific conditions based on the business context, allowing for centralized, fine-tuned access control through a user-friendly interface.

Augment SAP BTP applications with AMS

The following steps are necessary to develop a CAP application with AMS:

  1. Add the latest version of @sap/ams and @sap/ams-dev dependencies to package.json.
  2. Annotate your services and entities with standard @requires and @restrict CAP annotations for role-based access control (RBAC).
  3. (Optional) Add @ams.publicFields annotations to be able to write attribute-based access control (ABAC) policies in Data Control Language (DCL), the AMS-specific language used to express authorization policies.
  4. (Optional) Use @ams.alias annotations to decouple your entity design decisions from abstract resources used in your policies. This leaves you free to change your entities later without affecting existing policies.
  5. Run your application with cds watch. The ams development plugin (@sap/ams-dev) will compile your cds model along with the AMS annotations to Data Control Language (DCL). Alternatively, generate the DCL files manually via cds build --for ams (or during a full cds build).

    Now, you can see that in gen/srv/ams/dcl/cap/basePolicies.dcl the base policy admin and Reader is generated. A user with the policy cap.admin can access the endpoints of the AdminService, a user with the policy cap.Reader can see all the books from the CatalogService. 

    You can observe that a folder with name ams is generated in your root folder. This is where we can define our schema extensions and custom policies. By default, the folder is empty. In the following example, we define a custom policy JuniorReader in the cap folder. 

  6. The AMS runtime plugin (@sap/ams) enforces your authorization policies to secure the application. Once the application is deployed to Cloud Foundry, you can manage the AMS policies in the Cloud Identity Service admin console. Find the deployed application under Applications tab and assign policies to users under Authorization Policies. 

Summary

For newly built applications, using Authorization Management Service (AMS) is generally recommended. The primary requirement for using AMS is that users must be replicated to the Identity Directory Service, which serves as the central hub for managing users in SAP BTP applications. This aligns with SAP's broader strategy to simplify and streamline user management across its platform in the future. 

More information

  • Configuring Authorization Policies
  • Assign Authorization Policies
  • CAP Developer Guide
  • Github Samples

entry.content:

  • Devtoberfest sessio¡n calendar https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true 
  • Devtoberfest contest https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-contest/ba-p/13781593 

================================================================================ 

CHAPTER TITLES  

0:00 Intro 
0:07 SAP Cloud Application Programming Model September 2024 Release 
2:28 SAP Fiori Innovation Day 
3:10 SAP Cloud Integration News 
4:26 Flexso Tech Day 2024 
5:22 Devtoberfest Week 4 

TRANSCRIPT

[Ajay] Hello, developers, September 2024 release of CAP is now available. The first update is about analytics for Capire Matomo is used to collect anonymized usage data for Capire, and users will have the option to disable the tracking. Coming to the Node.js updates, you can now import type definitions generated by the CDS typer on the top level code, even in the test code. Now we have richer JavaScript and TypeScript snippets in the compiler, which can now carry type information, allowing you to get a better insight into the APIs used there. Instance-based restrictions for bound actions and functions are now also enforced. SAP Cloud Application Event Hub is the new default offering for messaging in SAP Business Technology Platform, Integrating with SAP Cloud Application Event Hub is provided via a CDS plugin @cap-js/event-broker. Moving on to Java updates, we have enhancement to the code generator, where we now have stricter setters in the accessor interfaces. Accessor interfaces now offer the new factory method to access the data in the given map. Monitoring health status of MTX Sidecar is enabled with a new health indicator model provider. Parameter aliases in OData V4 for the entity key values and function parameter values are now also supported. You can easily add handler stubs using add handler CLI command in Java. A new serious property, ui5.version allows you to configure UI5 versions used by the Fiori preview. Finally, in the tools update, we have got beta release to visualize deployment descriptor mta.yaml file. This helps you to get a graphical overview of your microservices architecture and the interplay between the application and the services. This also works as a visual linter. There are two new experimental CDS lint checks, sql-null-comparison and no-java-keywords. For more details, please check the links provided in the description.

[Nico] Hi everyone, and welcome to the SAP Developer News. The SAP Fiori Innovation Day will happen on November 19th in Heidelberg, Germany in the SAP App Haus, interestingly the same location we were at for SAP TechEd Virtual a couple of weeks ago. The SAP Fiori Innovation Day is open for everyone, and it will be a packed day with content about the SAP Fiori design system, SAP Fiori elements, AI of course, and it will also contain partner sessions. There will also be a hands-on session about the SAP Fiori elements flexible programming model. Check out the link in the description for more information and to register. See you. Bye.

[Antonio] Hola, SAP developers. I have some exciting news to share with you today. The message status overview that we are so used to in SAP PI/PO is now available in SAP Integration Suite. The message status overview page provides a customizable overview of the messages process and their statuses in a selected time frame, as well as quick navigation to and from the message monitoring in your tenant. Also, there is a new adapter available to us in Cloud Integration. This is the SMB adapter, which will allow you to connect to a server using the SMB protocol, meaning that we can interact with an SMB server and read and write files. This is especially relevant for those on-premises integration scenarios. And finally, there is now an alternative solution in Cloud Integration to the MessageTransformBean adapter module that we often use in SAP PI/PO to aid with the transformation of the message during the runtime. It is in the form of Groovy scripts, which support most of the features that existed on the MTB module. Make sure to check it out. Ciao.

[DJ] If you're in or can get to the Brussels / Mechelen area of Belgium in early November, then there's a Tech Day happening on the latest SAP tools and technologies. Flexo Tech Day 2024 is happening on Thursday, the 7th of November. And following the welcome and keynote, There are sessions across three separate tracks, analytics and integration, extensions, and there's also a trending track. Now, the full agenda is online. The day is open to everyone, but spaces are limited. So head on over to the website to register. And at the end of the day, there's gonna be a beer tasting, mostly because the day is being held in a brewery. What's not to like? I'll be there. See you there too.

[Nico] Hi everyone, and welcome to the SAP Developer News. We're just wrapping up week three of Devtoberfest, which means there's just one week left for Devtoberfest 2024. So next week we'll have five more days of packed content, all about SAP development. So on Monday, it will be ABAP and CAP again. On Tuesday, Tooling Tuesday, we'll talk about the SAP Build family. Wednesday is Integration Day. On Thursday we talk about MAD, which is machine learning, AI and data. And Friday will be Frontend Friday as well as Fun Friday. And for Fun Friday next week there will be Spooktoberfest, so expect a spooky day. Overall, next week I counted 24 sessions scheduled, that's a lot of content and it's great content too. We hope to see many more familiar faces as well as new faces. Welcome if you're new to this channel. Now's your chance to earn points in the Devtoberfest gameboard and to progress towards Nerdvana, which would qualify you for the Devtoberfest grand prize. So hope to see you around and see you next time.

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 New CodeJam: Expose capabilities of SAP AI Core with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model

0:55 TechEd On Tour

1:58 ML/AI in SAP Data Management products

3:58 The Open Source Way podcast episode: R/3 on Linux

Transcription

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for October 31st, 2024.

[Kevin] If you followed the announcements from SAP TechEd 2024, you know that there is a brand new SAP Cloud SDK for AI for JavaScript, Java, and ABAP. So I thought why not create a brand new code gem to teach you how to utilize AI capabilities within your CAP application using the SAP Cloud SDK for AI JavaScript version. So that code gem is out right now. So go ahead, visit the community.sap.com to see if there is a CodeJam happening right nearby you, or if you want to host one yourself. There is a ton of description on how to do that. And hopefully I'll see you next year in a CodeJam talking about AI and CAP. I can't wait.

[Promo] It's a long way from Philly to Heidelberg, but the show must go on. We're late, aren't we? Yeah, we're late. We all know there's nothing like the real thing. Luckily, we've got our best people on the job. Oh, sorry, Josh. Josh, if you pulled your own weight, we wouldn't have to carry you through this place.

[Witalij] We at SAP are enhancing our cloud native data management products with advanced machine learning and artificial intelligence technologies, providing robust tools for developers and data scientists. First, the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine announced at SAP TechEd Virtual is essential for developers and data scientists among you working with large language models or LLMs generator AI, as it facilitates the extraction of complex insights from bus data sets. Check Shabana's post to find out more.

Next, with the release of SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine, our development team built an insightful Movie Inside demo app to showcase the vector-based querying and storage capabilities. This demo illustrates the Vector Engine's role in enhancing LLM responses with contextual data. The entire demo is now available to you, providing a hands-on experience for developers and tech enthusiasts like you to explore the vector engine's capabilities and applications. To see such a practical implementation, check one of the partner applications known as SBF or Smart Budgeting and Forecasting.

Summarizing long documents and querying using the trivial augmented generation with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine, this application enables a quicker understanding of the attachments to budget request forms.

In addition, SAP DataSphere, in collaboration with NVIDIA, integrates Federated Machine Learning, or FEDML. This setup allows data scientists to conduct extensive ML experiments on SAP business data while ensuring data security and stellar performance.

Finally, the integration of data robots' AI models into SAP environments empowers developers and data scientists to embed AI-driven insights directly into their workflow. The partnership enhances the technical capabilities of SAP data management tools, making it easier to deploy and manage AI models within existing systems.

[DJ] Did you know that SAP, in particular the open source program office within SAP has a podcast show? It's been running now four years and to celebrate their fourth anniversary, they've published a monumentally epic episode. What is the episode about? Well, in my opinion, it's about one of the few major, major milestones in SAP software history, and that is the port of SAP R/3 to Linux. Now, you might think, oh, it's a big deal, but back then, and this was in the early 1990s, back then it was a massive deal. I was super lucky to be around at the time to get to know the two main actors in this affair, in this amazing event, Christoph Rohland and Harald Kuck. And these two, Christoph and Harald, are guests on this particular anniversary episode of the podcast show to talk about how it came to be, what happened. It's an amazing story. I can't recommend this episode strongly enough. Anyway, get the podcast show into your favourite podcast player, have a listen, and while you're at it, also have a look at this blog post from Ulrika, which talks a little bit more about the show, and also specifically about the milestone that Harold and Christoph will relate to you. So, see you online, and by the way, Happy birthday, the open-source way.
